text: Hello everyone, this is Roxen Liu from Oakland, California with a video on a left inguinal hernia repair where a large piece of retroperitoneal fat was discovered. This video is interesting because it shows the size of this piece of fat and how I reduced it.
I also got into just a little bit of bleeding problems in the retropubic space and just I'll talk through my strategy on how to deal with it.
The opening screen reveals a scrotal hernia containing a segment of the colon.
The surgeon examines the indirect inguinal hernia area after completing much of the initial dissection, revealing the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon carefully separates the normal fatty tissue surrounding the hernia sac at the internal ring, distinguishing it from the hernia sac itself.
The surgeon attempts to separate the vessels and retroperitoneal fat from the hernia sac during the left inguinal hernia repair.
The surgical field shows a significant amount of fatty tissue surrounding the area, with the surgeon still working to identify the leading edge of the hernia sac.
The surgeon identifies the edge of the hernia sac, which becomes more visible, while noting an additional piece of fat located on the medial side of the cord structure.
The surgeon proceeds with medial dissection past the midline, focusing on separating the easier tissue structures in the surgical field.
A small vessel is torn, resulting in a pooling of blood in the retropubic space. The surgeon attempts to manage the bleeding, which gradually increases.

Eventually, it pooled in the retropubic space, which makes it, of course, more dangerous to dissect down into the retropubic space due to potential for injury of the bladder and the obturator vessels and nerve and coronamortis.
Blood is visibly pooling in the surgical field as the surgeon attempts to control the bleeding using a bipolar cautery device. The focus is on reaching a large piece of fat in the area.

The surgeon dissects down to the pubis, encountering fatty tissue. The anatomical planes are not well-defined, with a large piece of medial vocal ligament fat laterally complicating the dissection process.

The surgeon observes a large clump of fat hanging off the epigastric vessel on the left side of the screen, which complicates the dissection. The hernia sac is visibly reduced within the surgical field.
The surgeon examines a scrotal hernia, noting the broad but shallow hernia sac. A piece of medial fat is identified as contributing to the external bulge, and the surgeon prepares to reduce it.
The testicular vessels are visibly pushed laterally, separated from the surrounding fat tissue.
The surgeon addresses a small bleeding vessel running up the fat on the medial side of the space while attempting to remove dangling fat from the inferior gastric region.
The surgeon identifies a separate piece of fat emerging from the inferior gastric region, distinct from the lateral retroperitoneal fat visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon demonstrates a clear plane of separation between two anatomical structures, reminiscent of the transverse salus sling that typically encases the vas deferens, although it is not that structure.
The surgeon identifies and divides vessels and lymphatics within the tissue to achieve complete reduction of the hernia.
The surgeon navigates through natural tissue planes, avoiding bleeding while addressing a pool of accumulated blood. Lymphatics are visible, and the surgeon plans to clip them later.

The surgeon dissects two centimeters deep into the retropubic space to obtain the critical view of the myopectic ulcer. The transfer salus sling is visible, and the dissection is performed to the right of the midline, aiming to reach the correct plane without encountering bleeding.

The surgeon navigates towards a clean plane on the right side, away from the pool of blood, ensuring the blood remains contained and does not infiltrate the clean area. This strategy allows safer dissection through the bloody tissue.
The surgeon identifies a clear anatomical plane and uses it to safely dissect deeper into the surgical field, moving from a known area to an unknown region.
The surgeon continues to prioritize the cord, working within a clearly defined natural tissue plane while demonstrating effective hemostasis to control bleeding.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis, revealing the plane between the medial molecular fat and the vas deferens. The shiny fat to the right of the bleeding area is identified as the obturator nodes, which are kept behind while prioritizing the cord.

The surgeon observes that the vas deferens is distant from the current plane of dissection and plans to insert a piece of gauze to facilitate dissection near the medial and vocal ligament, which is positioned behind the vas deferens.

The surgeon carefully peels the fat off the vas deferens and testicular vessel, preparing to insert a piece of mesh. The mesh is tucked vertically into the retropubic space to cover the obturator nodes and as much of the vas as possible, preventing fat from slipping underneath.
The surgeon exposes the obturator node and creates a small window to reveal Cooper's ligament for suturing. The wide obturator node packet is visible as the surgeon prepares the area for sewing.

The surgeon identifies a tubular structure in the area where the medial umbilical ligament would typically be located. Due to distorted anatomy, the surgeon is uncertain whether the structure is the medial umbilical ligament or the ureter, and decides to leave it undisturbed.

The surgeon mobilizes the surrounding fat and applies a clip to secure divided lymphatics and vessels near a tubular structure. Attempts are made to trace the tubular structure along the medial umbilical ligament fold, but complete tracing is not achieved.

The surgeon lifts a structure that tethers the medial umbilical ligament fold, confirming its identity as the medial umbilical ligament. The vas deferens is visible, positioned laterally due to the large medial umbilical ligament path.

The surgeon identifies a large mass of medial umbilical ligament fat, which is unusually positioned medially and displaces the testicular vessels. The surgical field shows more bleeding than usual as the surgeon navigates around small vessels.

In hindsight, I think it was because my fellow had started this case and his energy setting for the monopolar was different. And so with my technique of using the scissor like a bovie, I think it wasn't quite sealing all the vessels as I was kind of boving.

The surgeon requests a mega suture cut needle driver to prepare for mesh placement. A large defect is visible with a lymph node adjacent to it. The surgeon clips a previously divided lymphatic channel.
The surgeon observes a fatty packet containing lymphatic tissue left behind after reducing the fat. A Raytech sponge is used to blot blood near the medial umbilical ligament.

The surgeon uses a 3D Max mid left side mesh, already placed in the abdomen before docking the robot, to address the defect. The mesh is positioned while ensuring the peritoneum on the testicular vessels is managed.

The surgeon tucks the mesh securely to prevent the fatty packet from herniating underneath. A varicose suture is placed to Cooper's ligament to ensure the mesh remains in place, as the large defect allows the mesh to potentially billow out and lift at the lower edge.
The surgeon places additional sutures around the large defect to secure the mesh, ensuring it remains in position and does not protrude through the defect.
The surgeon secures the mesh around the internal ring, ensuring it is tightly positioned. The transverse salus is placated or sewn down to Cooper's ligament to address direct defects.
The surgeon pauses to capture images of the reduced fat, which is visibly significant in size. The previously palpable mass included part of the colon as a slider within the internal ring.
The surgeon inserts a suction cannula into the surgical site, allowing intra-abdominal pressure to expel CO₂ and rapidly desufflate the pocket during the final stages of inguinal hernia repair.
